title: auto should be avoided as a storage class specifier
description: "auto" should not be used as a storage class specifierAnalyze your codeintentionality-logicalmaintainabilityCode Smelllock-inWhy is this an issue?Before C++11,autowas used as a storage class specifier that indicated automatic duration. Since that’s the default, the use ofautoin that context was wholly redundant.Because the keyword was redundant and therefore rarely used, C++11 repurposes it.autois now used to specify that the type of the variable or function should be deduced from its context.Since it is redundant under older standards and problematic under C++11,auto's use as a storage-class identifier should be removed.Noncompliant code exampleauto int x; // Noncompliant: redundant before C++11, error as of C++11 auto int y; // NoncompliantCompliant solutionint x; auto y = 1 + 2; // C++1
recommendation: Remove the problematic code and refactor as needed.

noncompliant: auto int x = 10; // Noncompliant: auto as storage class specifier
auto char buffer[100]; // Noncompliant: redundant auto keyword
compliant: int x = 10; // Compliant: explicit type declaration
auto y = 1 + 2; // Compliant: auto for type deduction (C++11+)
char buffer[100]; // Compliant: no redundant auto

checks.message: auto should be avoided as a storage class specifier - use explicit type or modern auto type deduction.
